title: Fixed #36985 -- Skipped CreateExtension backwards operation on non-PostgreSQL databases.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36985 

#### Branch description
If we try to reverse a CreateExtension migration on non postgres databases, Django crashes and throws an OperationalError.

We are fixing this by adding a database check in database_backwards() to see if the database is actually PostgreSQL.

title: Fixed #36960 -- Enabled the use of psycopg 3's optimized timestamp loader.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-36960

#### Branch description

Even when available via psycopg-c or psycopg-binary, Django would never use the optimized C implementation of timestamp loaders.

This changes the implementation to load a reference to the actual registered timestamptz loader class from psycopg instead of deriving from the always-Python impl.

This was based on Daniele Varrazzo's code from https://github.com/psycopg/psycopg/issues/1273#issuecomment-3986829769.

On my machine, this speeds up a `assert list(SomeModel.objects.values_list("ctime", flat=True))` benchmark (where the length of the list ends up being some 100,000 items) with psycopg-binary==3.3.3 from 12.543 s ± 0.084 s to 4.647 s ± 0.039 s.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)

- [x] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [ ] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

#### Checklist
- [x] This PR follows the [contribution guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/).
- [x] This PR **does not** disclose a security vulnerability (see [vulnerability reporting](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/security/)).
- [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period (see [guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/committing-code/#committing-guidelines)).
- [x] I have not requested, and will not request, an automated AI review for this PR. <!-- You are welcome to do so in your own fork. -->
- [x] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system.
- [ ] I have added or updated relevant tests.
  - No tests needed, I don't think? (One could add a test to check that the class we borrow is the optimized one if the test is being run with optimized classes available, but eh...).  
- [ ] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable
  - Not yet. Does this need doc updates? Of course it'd be nice to note that psycopg 3 support is better and faster.
- [ ] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: akx

reviews.body: Thanks!

Fixed #36960 -- Enabled the use of psycopg 3's optimized timestamp loader.

Based on Daniele Varrazzo's comment in https://github.com/psycopg/psycopg/issues/1273#issuecomment-3986829769

reviews.body:
The change to `UUIDField.get_db_prep_value` looks good to me. As for the test, the purpose of that custom field was to provide a PK field whose `get_prep_value` call and "from db" call (done via a field's `from_db_value` or the field/connection's `get_db_converters()`) would have type mismatches. Replacing the `get_prep_value` override with `get_db_prep_value` wouldn't work for the regression test.

I double checked by detaching to d5c19f9b327fccc1f707e10de648fe9d4cdacb9b, reverting the fix, and checking whether the regression test fails with this version of CustomUUIDField.
🏆 Thanks

commits.message:
Fixed #36987 -- Observed prepared argument in UUIDField.get_db_prep_value().

This avoids two isinstance() calls per UUID value.

title: Discouraged automated AI reviews of pull requests.
body:
#### Branch description

Some contributors have been requesting Copilot reviews on pull requests submitted to the Django repository. These automated reviews add noise to the PR history, making it harder to follow the discussion, and Django does not incorporate them into its review process.

This change discourages the practice by:

- Adding a `copilot-instructions.md` file instructing Copilot to decline review requests in this repository.
- Adding a checklist item to the PR template asking contributors not to request automated AI reviews for their PR.
- Documenting the policy in the contributing guidelines.

Contributors who find automated AI reviews useful are encouraged to open a PR against their own fork and request the review there before submitting to the Django repository. 

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [X] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

Claude (sonnet 4.6) was used to investigate which options were available to disable copilot reviews.

#### Checklist
- [X] This PR follows the [contribution guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/).
- [X] This PR **does not** disclose a security vulnerability (see [vulnerability reporting](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/security/)).
- [X] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [X] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period.
- [X] I have not requested, and will not request, an automated AI review for this PR. <!-- You are welcome to do so in your own fork. -->
- [ ] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system.
- [ ] I have added or updated relevant tests.
- [X] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable.
- [ ] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: nessita

commits.message:
Discouraged automated AI reviews of pull requests.

Some contributors have been requesting Copilot reviews on pull requests
submitted to the Django repository. These automated reviews add noise to
the PR history, making it harder to follow the discussion, and Django
does not incorporate them into its review process.

This change discourages the practice by:

- Adding a `copilot-instructions.md` file instructing Copilot to decline
  review requests in this repository.
- Adding a checklist item to the PR template asking contributors not to
  request automated AI reviews for their PR.
- Documenting the policy in the contributing guidelines.

title: Fixed #36885 -- Fixed long verbose names in admin filter sidebar breaking layout
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36885

#### Branch description
Long verbose names in the admin filter sidebar `<summary>` elements had no `word-break` rule, causing them to overflow the sidebar's fixed width and squeeze the main changelist content area. Extended `word-break: break-word` to cover summary alongside `a`, and added `overflow: hidden` to the sidebar container as a safeguard.

title: [6.0.x] Updated translations from Transifex.
body:
#### Branch description
Update translations from Transifex, following the [instructions](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/6.0/internals/howto-release-django/#a-few-days-before-any-release). We do this roughly once between feature releases. Two strings had to be fixed on Transifex before exporting.

#### Questions
- `Plural-Forms` for Hebrew in the fetched files are now 3, not 4. Although we normally revert changes to an _increasing_ number of plural forms, which I have done in this case for Portuguese(pt), a _reduction_ in plural forms is probably good? See at least one existing file that already committed this change. The others that haven't seen recent edits are still at 4:
https://github.com/django/django/blob/9c21dd91a3bc5d4278adfff98c7a5225947cd049/django/contrib/postgres/locale/he/LC_MESSAGES/django.po#L19-L20
Are we fine with continuing to follow this transition to fewer plural forms?
- In a separate commit (for now, just for review) I edited the `Plural-Forms` for Portuguese(pt) to stay at 2 instead of increasing to 3, and deleted the corresponding extra forms that already had entries. I regenerated the corresponding *.mo file with `msgfmt -c -o <path.mo> <path.po>`. Is that correct?

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [x] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [ ] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

title: Fixed #36906 -- Handled coalescing JSON-primitive strings and JSON values on Oracle.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

[ticket-36906](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/36906#comment:2)

#### Branch description
In Oracle database, type was strict. when we performed `Coalesce`, it could not mix between type. Previously, we didn't support type guard for `JSONField`. So, it was casted as `NCLOB`. It will break database guard if we mix it with other type such as `VARCHAR` described in ticket.

I added guard to cast JSONField to `NCLOB`.

Good additional context: https://github.com/django/django/pull/20864#discussion_r2932374547

reviews.body: Hey David, thanks for the patch! Please review the [contributing guidelines ](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/) to make sure everything is in order:

Thanks 👍 

I confirmed with VoiceOver that toggling the checkboxes now announces "checked, 1 of 2 selected" instead of just "checked".

Will wait to merge to see if accessibility team wants to have a look.

Thanks, I audited the other `textContent` cases mentioned on the ticket.

I added `aria-live="polite"` and `aria-atomic="true"` to the filtered-selected-items warning in `SelectFilter2`, since that message updates while users filter the chosen list.

I left the other cases unchanged:
- `DateTimeShortcuts` inserts its warning once on page load.
- `calendar.js` reads `textContent`; it doesn’t update status text.
- `RelatedObjectLookups` updates labels/values rather than a status message.

On `aria-atomic`, I agree it may not be strictly necessary when we replace the whole `.textContent`, but I kept it because it was part of the ticket’s proposed fix and seems harmless/consistent here.

title: Provided script for preparing commit messages and updated relevant docs (two commits).
body:
#### Branch description
Follow up to #20381. This branch adds a python script (scripts/prepare_commit_msg.py) ready to be used as a `prepare-commit-msg` git hook.

Hooking and using this script is optional, and if installed, it will automatically format commit messages as follows:

- Adds the `[A.B.x]` branch prefix if missing when committing on stable branches.
- Ensures the summary line starts with a capital letter (modulo stable prefix) and ends with a period.
- Appends `Backport of <sha> from main.` when cherry-picking, even after conflict resolution.

The hook is designed as an independent alternative to the existing `scripts/backport.sh` as a more robust alternative. It handles cherry-pick conflicts transparently, whereas the shell script requires manual message formatting after conflict resolution.

The core logic lives in a python helper function `process_commit_message()`. Tests are included inside the `scripts/` folder since that directory is pruned from the distribution artifact. Tests are runnable using `PYTHONPATH=scripts/ python -m unittest scripts/tests.py` on demand.

Installation and usage instructions are documented in the new `Backports` section of  the existing committing guidelines docs.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [X] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

Claude (sonnet 4.6) was used to develop the script and write tests for it, I conducted a review of this code and also performed manual testing given various committing scenarios.

title: Fixed #36968 -- Improved error message when collectstatic can't find a referenced file.
body:
#### Trac ticket number ticket-36968 #### Branch description A suggestion of how we could improve the message that is shown to people when the file referenced in a css/js is missing. Wraps the ValueError for missing file when it comes from collectstatic in a 'CommandError' and updated the message to include a little more information. The CommandError will hide the traceback by default and it can still be accessed by providing --traceback Example
Vs Old
Open to feedback on wording. It's inspired by [whitenoise](https://github.com/evansd/whitenoise/blob/13997326100d37bc0b9edab01c886dea85bc05c3/src/whitenoise/storage.py#L190C18-L190C34) The intent is to make it easier for people to find where the issue is in their files when things go wrong. But where there is a suggestion on what to look for in whitenoise, I felt it was safer to give the person the information of the file missing, the file and line it was found in, and leave it for them to fix it from there. It could be a number of things that need fixing, from most to least likely in my experience 1) An error in your code i.ie. a typo, wrong folder, or actual missing file 2) external lib references file you don't have/need, e.g. sourcemap file 3) confusion over relative paths and how storage resolves files 4) storage is not configured correctly and not picking up files you expect it to Trying to call out any one of these could mislead people, calling out them all is too long, and it's probably not exhaustive anyway. #### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED) - [X] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR. - [ ] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output. #### Checklist - [X] This PR follows the [contribution guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/). - [X] This PR **does not** disclose a security vulnerability (see [vulnerability reporting](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/security/)). - [X] This PR targets the `main` branch. - [X] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period. - [X] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system. - [X] I have added or updated relevant tests. - [X] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable. title: Fixed #36958 -- Reloaded logging config when logging settings are changed in tests.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36958

#### Branch description
Currently, when the `LOGGING` or `LOGGING_CONFIG` settings change during tests, such as through `@override_settings`, the test client does not adjust the Python logging module to align with the new settings.

This PR fixes the problem by adding a `setting_changed` signal receiver to `django/test/signals.py`. The receiver automatically calls `configure_logging(settings.LOGGING_CONFIG, settings.LOGGING)` whenever these settings are changed.

A regression test has also been added to `tests/logging_tests/tests.py` to ensure that the logger's level updates correctly when the setting is overridden.

title: Aligned docs checks between GitHub Actions and local development.
body:
#### Branch description
Previously, each docs check ran as an isolated CI job with its own setup, but the logic was duplicated from the Makefile targets without sharing configuration. This meant CI and local development would silently diverge, both for options passed and for versions used. Centralizing everything in make check ensures that any future changes to how checks are run only need to happen in one place.

title: Encapsulated loop logic to avoid leaking module-level variables.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

None, trivial change

#### Branch description
While working on django-stubs, and following PR-20760, we noticed that the loop variables `d`, `connector`, `field_types`, `lhs`, `rhs`, and `result` leak into the `expressions` module namespace after the `register_combinable_fields` initialization loop. This means users can accidentally import `expressions.connector` or `expressions.d` which are just leftover loop values, not part of the API. It also forces django-stubs to add these to the stubtest [allowlist](https://github.com/emmanuel-ferdman/django-stubs/blob/73987cc183b3021c2a44b8e17678057774daf113/scripts/stubtest/allowlist_todo.txt#L407). This one-liner change removes them.

title: Fixed #22079 -- Added tests for empty iterable stripping in RequestFactory.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-22079

#### Branch description

The original issue reported inconsistent handling of empty list values between GET and POST in the test client — POST stripped them while GET preserved them.

Both GET and POST now consistently strip empty list/tuple values from request data. This PR adds regression tests to `RequestFactoryTest` documenting this consistent behavior for:
- GET with empty list values
- GET with empty tuple values
- POST with empty list values
- POST with empty tuple values

reviews.body:
Thanks, only a minor question for you.

I wonder if we should mock `{"DJANGO_COLORS": "nocolor"}` instead:
- to represent that the test specifically requires no color, not agnosticism about color
- to leave a smaller footprint on the environment

commits.message:
Refs #36936 - Adjusted tests to set PYTHON_COLORS environment variable.

When "FORCE_COLOR" was set in the environment (such as in django-docker-box)
several test cases that rely on string comparisons against exception
messages or command line results would fail due to ANSI escape sequences
being present in the output. This change resolved the issue by setting
the "PYTHON_COLORS" environment variable to "0" for those specific tests,
which takes precendence over the presence of "FORCE_COLOR".

title: Fixed #36946 -- Respected test database name when running tests in parallel on SQLite.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36946

#### Branch description

The code changes are the same as the patch provided on the ticket.

When connecting to the cloned databases, we should use the database name that was previously generated using `get_test_db_clone_settings()` (which takes into account the `NAME` set in `TEST`), instead of hardcoding the file name using the `{alias}_{_worker_id}.sqlite3` format.

With the following changes to the test settings:

```diff
diff --git a/tests/test_sqlite.py b/tests/test_sqlite.py
index 0e6b0f672a..77981218fd 100644
--- a/tests/test_sqlite.py
+++ b/tests/test_sqlite.py
@@ -29,3 +29,9 @@ PASSWORD_HASHERS = [
 ]

 USE_TZ = False
+
+import os
+
+DATABASE_NAME = os.getenv("DATABASE_NAME")
+if DATABASE_NAME:
+    DATABASES["default"]["TEST"] = {"NAME": DATABASE_NAME}
```

you can see that running a subset of the Django test suite with the following command:

```sh
DATABASE_NAME=django.db ./runtests.py --verbosity=2 --parallel=2 -- model_fields
```

will now pass. Without this PR, it will fail with an error as described in the ticket.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [x] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

No AI tools were used for writing the implementation changes.

GitHub Copilot on VSCode with Claude Sonnet 4.5 was used to:

- Confirm where to write the test. I already identified it as `test_creation` by looking at #15421, but I thought I'd ask AI to see if it would come up with the same result. It did!
- Write an initial version of the test. I ended up rewriting pretty much the whole test, because AI failed to recognise that `setup_worker_connection()` is meant to be called from the child process which already has the connection settings set to the `TEST` dictionary. It wrote an assertion that would happily pass with the old implementation that resulted in `default_2.sqlite3` instead of `mytest_2.db`.


#### Checklist

- [x] This PR **does not** disclose a security vulnerability (see [vulnerability reporting](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/security/)).

title: Deleted leaking loop iter vars in `smartif.py`.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

None, trivial change

#### Branch description

While working on https://github.com/typeddjango/django-stubs/pull/3114/changes#diff-c408a224a641ee1ad26789d61b19c64c44667c1feb8fce0d58aea0338844ca66R276-R278 we have noticed that loop variables leak into the module namespace.

It has two negative effects:
1. Users can access `smartif.op` and `smartif.key` public attrs, which is clearly not intenteded
2. We in `django-stubs` have to ignore these two objects from annotations

Let's remove them :)

commits.message:
Delete leaking loop iter vars in `smartif.py`

While working on https://github.com/typeddjango/django-stubs/pull/3114/changes#diff-c408a224a641ee1ad26789d61b19c64c44667c1feb8fce0d58aea0338844ca66R276-R278 we have noticed that loop variables leak into the module namespace.

Let's remove them :)

title: Fixed #36939 -- Avoided weakref.finalize in Signal.connect().
body:
Replaced weak receiver registration in Signal.connect() to pass _flag_dead_receivers directly as the callback for weakref.ref() and weakref.WeakMethod() instead of creating weakref.finalize() objects.

This prevented finalizer accumulation in repeated weak connect()/disconnect() cycles where receivers remain alive.

#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36939

#### Branch description

Avoid using weakref.finalize() when connecting signals since they leak.
 
Replaced weak receiver registration in Signal.connect() to pass
_flag_dead_receivers directly as the callback for weakref.ref() and
weakref.WeakMethod() instead of creating weakref.finalize() objects.
 
This prevented finalizer accumulation in repeated weak
connect()/disconnect() cycles where receivers remain alive.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure

<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [x] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

Claude was used to pinpoint the issue that lead to the leak which was `weakref.finalize()`. It then suggested to keep track of the return values from `weakref.finalize()` and to `detach()` them when `disconnect()ing` signals.

Instead of accepting those changes a much simpler fix was discovered to discard `weakref.finalize()` usages all together and just use the ref's callback to `_flag_dead_receivers`.

Worth noting @charettes already added a similar `weakref.ref(..., self._flag_dead_receivers)` call in the block below back in 760121dcb1837fdee6ac7f4e2412c0d539fbff7a.

commits.message:
Fixed #36939 -- Avoided weakref.finalize in Signal.connect().

Replaced weak receiver registration in Signal.connect() to pass
_flag_dead_receivers directly as the callback for weakref.ref() and
weakref.WeakMethod() instead of creating weakref.finalize() objects.

This prevented finalizer accumulation in repeated weak
connect()/disconnect() cycles where receivers remain alive.

title: Fixed #36940 -- Improved ASGI script prefix path_info handling.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-36940

#### Branch description

The current `ASGIRequest.__init__` uses `str.removeprefix()` to strip the script name from the request path to compute `path_info`. This is fragile because `removeprefix` is a pure string operation — it doesn't verify that the prefix is a proper path segment boundary.

For example, if `script_name` is `/myapp` and the path is `/myapplication/page`, `removeprefix` would incorrectly produce `lication/page`.

This patch replaces `removeprefix` with a check that ensures the script name is followed by `/` or is the exact path, before stripping it. This addresses the inline TODO comment.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
- [x] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

AI tools (Claude) were used to understand the issue and guide the approach. The code was reviewed and verified manually.

title: Fixed #36937 -- Added missing positive permission tests in test_client.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
Ticket: [https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/36937](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/36937)

#### Branch description
This PR resolves two standing `TODO` comments in `tests/test_client/tests.py` regarding missing positive test coverage for the `@permission_required` decorator (`test_view_with_permissions` and `test_view_with_method_permissions`).

**Rationale and Changes:**
* **`tests/test_client/views.py`**: The dummy views were originally decorated with a malformed permission string (`"permission_not_granted"`). Because this string lacked a standard `app_label`, Django's standard `ModelBackend.has_perm()` failed to parse it during authorization checks. I updated the dummy permission string to a standard format (`"auth.add_user"`) to allow for proper evaluation.
* **`tests/test_client/tests.py`**: With the views properly configured, I implemented the positive test paths. The tests now retrieve the real `auth.add_user` permission via the ORM, assign it to the test user, and successfully assert the `200 OK` response.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)

title: Fixed #36935 -- Added fallback in ContentType.app_labeled_name when model_class() is None.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-36935

#### Branch description

Updated `ContentType.app_labeled_name` to include `app_label` when `model_class()` returns `None`. Previously, the fallback returned only `self.model`, which could lead to ambiguity when multiple applications share the same database or when stale content types reference models not present in the current codebase.

The new behavior returns `"app_label | model"` in this case, aligning the fallback representation with the labeled format used when the model class is available.

Related tests were updated to reflect the new string representation.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)

title: Fixed #36931 -- Handled LookupError in multipart parser for invalid RFC 2231 encoding
body:
Added LookupError to the except clause so invalid headers are silently skipped, consistent with other malformed header handling.

#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36931

#### Branch description

[parse_header_parameters()](https://github.com/django/django/blob/main/django/utils/http.py#L332) in [django/utils/http.py](https://github.com/django/django/blob/main/django/utils/http.py) passes an invalid encoding name to `urllib.parse.unquote()` when processing RFC 2231 encoded parameters (e.g., `filename*=BOGUS''test%20file.txt`). When the encoding is invalid and the value contains percent-encoded characters, `unquote()` raises `LookupError`.

The `except ValueError` clause in [parse_boundary_stream()](https://github.com/django/django/blob/main/django/http/multipartparser.py#L729) (`multipartparser.py:729`) does not catch `LookupError` (`issubclass(LookupError, ValueError)` is `False`), causing an unhandled 500 Internal Server Error.

This patch adds `LookupError` to the except clause so the invalid header is silently skipped, consistent with how other malformed headers are handled. A regression test is included.

Confirmed on Django 6.0.2, Python 3.14.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [x] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

Gemini was used.

title: Refs #36494 -- Prevented crash in JSONField numeric lookups with expressions.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36494

#### Branch description
The ORM correctly handles dynamic expressions on the right-hand side by bypassing JSON deserialization for non-string objects.

Previously, ``KeyTransformNumericLookupMixin.process_rhs()`` attempted to call ``json.loads()`` on all parameters when native JSON support was missing. This resulted in a ``TypeError`` or ``JSONDecodeError`` when encountering expression objects. The implemented defensive check and fallback mirror the established pattern in ``JSONField.from_db_value()``.
Used Gemini to analyze the ticket and formulate the implementation strategy.

title: Added DatabaseFeatures.pattern_lookup_needs_param_pattern.
body:
It's useful on MongoDB.

It allows this cleanup: 
```diff
diff --git a/django_mongodb_backend/lookups.py b/django_mongodb_backend/lookups.py
index 73b46d7d..2644c458 100644
--- a/django_mongodb_backend/lookups.py
+++ b/django_mongodb_backend/lookups.py
@@ -139,15 +139,6 @@ def pattern_lookup_prep_lookup_value(self, value):
         # Analogous to PatternLookup.get_rhs_op() / pattern_esc.
         for find, replacement in REGEX_MATCH_ESCAPE_CHARS:
             value = {"$replaceAll": {"input": value, "find": find, "replacement": replacement}}
-    else:
-        # If value is a literal, remove percent signs added by
-        # PatternLookup.process_rhs() for LIKE queries.
-        if self.lookup_name in ("startswith", "istartswith"):
-            value = value[:-1]
-        elif self.lookup_name in ("endswith", "iendswith"):
-            value = value[1:]
-        elif self.lookup_name in ("contains", "icontains"):
-            value = value[1:-1]
     return value
```

title: Fixed #36927 -- Optimized Field.deconstruct().
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-36927

#### Branch description

This PR optimizes this function in two ways:

1. Flattening the loop over attributes and default values. The resulting "less clever" code is IMO clearer as to what it does.
2. Removing the chain of `str.startswith()` comparisons for path reduction. This may be less clear, but each `startswith()` was kinda expensive, especially since they all redundantly checked the common prefix `django.db.models.fields.`

To measure the effect of my changes, I patched a [tprof](https://github.com/adamchainz/tprof) call into `runtests.py`:

```diff
@@ -406,7 +406,10 @@ def django_tests(
         shuffle=shuffle,
         durations=durations,
     )
-    failures = test_runner.run_tests(test_labels)
+    from tprof import tprof
+
+    with tprof("django.db.models.fields:Field.deconstruct"):
+        failures = test_runner.run_tests(test_labels)
     teardown_run_tests(state)
     return failures
```

…and ran a bunch of ORM-using tests:

```
$ ./runtests.py --parallel 1 model* queries expressions*
```

Before:

```
🎯 tprof results:
 function                                    calls total mean ± σ   min … max
 django.db.models.fields:Field.deconstruct() 12325  79ms  6μs ± 1μs 5μs … 52μs
```

After:

```
🎯 tprof results:
 function                                    calls total mean ± σ   min … max
 django.db.models.fields:Field.deconstruct() 12325  55ms  4μs ± 2μs 3μs … 41μs
```

The results show a ~30% speedup.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [x] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

I used GitHub Copilot autocomplete to fill in some of the repetitive lines, and a review from Anthropic Claude to verify accuracy.

title: Fixed #36839 -- Warned when model renames encounter conflicts from stale ContentTypes.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-36839

#### Branch description

Fixed #36839 -- Warn when ContentType rename conflicts with stale entries.

When renaming a model, Django automatically injects a `RenameContentType` operation to update the content type. If a stale content type with the target name already exists, the operation catches the `IntegrityError` and silently reverts without notifying the user. This results in the migration appearing successful while the content type rename actually failed, leading to broken GenericForeignKey references and stale data.

This change adds a `RuntimeWarning` when the rename conflict occurs, informing users to run the `remove_stale_contenttypes` management command to resolve the issue. The existing fallback behavior is preserved—no data is deleted or overwritten automatically.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)

Claude Sonnet 4.5 was used for assistance with adding tests. All output was thoroughly reviewed and verified by me.

title: Fixed #36894 -- Added TypeError for conflicting arguments in mail APIs.
body:
A TypeError is now raised if fail_silently=True, auth_user, or auth_password are provided along a connection.

Updated AdminEmailHandler in django.utils.log to remove redundant fail_silently=True.

Thanks, Mike Edmunds for the report and Jacob Tyler Walls for the review.

#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36894

#### Branch description
This PR addresses the issue where connection-related arguments (fail_silently, auth_user, auth_password) are silently ignored when an explicit connection is passed.

Following the suggested fix in the ticket I did the following things:
- changed the default of fail_silently to None across the mail API to detect explicit user input.
- added TypeError validation to prevent conflicting arguments when a connection is provided.
- updated AdminEmailHandler in django.utils.log to remove redundant fail_silently=True.
- added regression tests and updated documentation with versionchanged notes.
- adjusted existing tests in MailDeprecatedPositionalArgsTests to use None for connection-related arguments when an explicit connection is provided.

title: Optimized SQLite `DatabaseOperations.check_expression_support()`.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

N/A

#### Branch description

Avoided reconstructing the same tuples on every call by defining them as module-level constants.

The tuples have been made within the method since eb6c107624930c97390185fdbf7f887c50665808. It’s a pretty hot function as it’s called for every expression, so it’s worth improving.

I measured the improvement by adding a call to [tprof](https://github.com/adamchainz/tprof) within `runtests.py`:

```diff
@@ -406,7 +406,11 @@ def django_tests(
         shuffle=shuffle,
         durations=durations,
     )
-    failures = test_runner.run_tests(test_labels)
+
+    from django.db.backends.sqlite3.operations import DatabaseOperations
+    from tprof import tprof
+    with tprof(DatabaseOperations.check_expression_support):
+        failures = test_runner.run_tests(test_labels)
     teardown_run_tests(state)
     return failures
```

…and running the expression tests:

```
$ ./runtests.py --parallel 1 expressions
```

Before:

```
🎯 tprof results:
 function                                                                            calls total mean ± σ     min … max
 django.db.backends.sqlite3.operations:DatabaseOperations.check_expression_support()   793 809μs  1μs ± 2μs 708ns … 55μs
```

After:

```
🎯 tprof results:
 function                                                                            calls total  mean ± σ       min … max
 django.db.backends.sqlite3.operations:DatabaseOperations.check_expression_support()   793 648μs 817ns ± 945ns 583ns … 26μs
```

By comparing the total times, we can see a 648/809 ≈ 20% reduction in time spent in `check_expression_support()`.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [x] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

Copilot autocomplete.

#### Checklist
- [x] This PR follows the [contribution guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/).
- [x] This PR **does not** disclose a security vulnerability (see [vulnerability reporting](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/security/)).
- [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period.
- [x] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system.
- [x] I have added or updated relevant tests.
- [x] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable.
- [x] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: adamchainz

reviews.body: Thank you!

title: Clarified optional nature of Contributor License Agreement.
body:
It's not clear that CLAs are needed to ensure contributors are assenting to our license (the "inbound=outbound" agreement), but we can keep them around for contributors who would like to (or are required by their employer) to submit one, without investing additional resources in checking every single contribution.

See https://forum.djangoproject.com/t/cla-vs-dco-for-django-contributors/42399 and recent board minutes.

commits.message:
Clarified optional nature of Contributor License Agreement.

It's not clear that CLAs are needed to ensure contributors are
assenting to our license (the "inbound=outbound" agreement),
but we can keep them around for contributors who would like to
(or are required by their employer) to submit one, without
investing additional resources in checking every single contribution.

See https://forum.djangoproject.com/t/cla-vs-dco-for-django-contributors/42399
and recent board minutes.

title: Refs #35961 -- add AUTHORS back to wheel
body:
As a side-effect from adding explicit license files in 96a7a652166bece8acc96d6335ebb8091de2f496, which is required to conform to PEP 639, the AUTHORS file got dropped from the Django wheel. The tarball still contained this file.

@jacobtylerwalls found that in the "Python Packaging User Guide" at https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/writing-pyproject-toml/#license-files the AUTHORS file is shown as to be specified as part of license-files.

I added it there and confirmed that AUTHORS is now again part of the wheel.

#### Trac ticket number
ticket-35961

#### Branch description
follow-up fix for the original commit

to conform to PEP 639, the AUTHORS file got dropped from the
Django wheel. The tarball still contained this file.

Jacob Walls found that in the "Python Packaging User Guide"
at https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/writing-pyproject-toml/#license-files
the AUTHORS file is shown as to be specified as part of license-files.

I changed pyproject.toml accordingly and confirmed that AUTHORS is
now again part of the wheel.

title: Fixed #36893 -- Serialized elidable kwarg for RunSQL and RunPython operations.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36893

#### Branch description

Fixed an issue where `elidable=True` was not being serialized for Run-SQL and Run-Python operations which caused these operations to lose their elidable status when written to a migration file (e.g., during squashing), reverting to the default `False`.

-Updated deconstruct() for both Run-SQL and Run-Python to correctly include 'elidable' keyword when set to 'True' 
-Regression Tests are test_run_sql_elidable , test_run_python_elidable and test_operations.py

title: [4.2.x] Refs #36499 -- Adjusted test_strip_tags to run on Python 3.8.
body:
See:
```py
======================================================================
ERROR: test_strip_tags (utils_tests.test_html.TestUtilsHtml)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/jwalls/django/tests/utils_tests/test_html.py", line 105, in test_strip_tags
    sys.version_info >= min_fixed_security[sys.version_info[:2]]
KeyError: (3, 8)

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 4 tests in 0.027s
```

The `KeyError` was already fixed on main by using `.get()`, but it appears `.get()` didn't make it into the conflict resolution when backporting.

Anyway, we also needed to update the second block of version information to reflect that 3.8 didn't have an update there either.

title: Added documentation on reviewing patches.
body:
This was primarily sourced from Sarah Boyce's talk, "Django needs you! (to do code review)"
Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MBqampUQ1o0

#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

N/A

#### Branch description
This adds a brief overview of how to review patches to the documentation. Having something defined for doing code reviews should make this part of the software development process more accessible to other developers.

title: Refs #35744 -- Removed problematic __in lookup in test_intersection_in_nested_subquery.
body:
It's problematic on MongoDB.

[Simon](https://github.com/django/django/pull/18555#discussion_r2729349464): "It seems odd that we'd use __in=OuterRef("pk") over __in=[OuterRef("pk")]. It's a SQLism that only works because right-hand-side is wrapped with (...) and that's interpreted as a singleton tuple which is allowed with IN."
author: timgraham

__in=OuterRef("pk") over __in=[OuterRef("pk")]. It's a SQLism that
only works because right-hand-side is wrapped with (...) and that's
interpreted as a singleton tuple which is allowed with IN."

title: Fixed #36795 -- Enforced quoting of all database object names.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36795

#### Branch description

Currenly trying to learn more about why we started not quoting users probided aliases as 9c52d56f6f8a9cdafb231adf9f4110473099c9b5 which introduced the change has very little details about its origin.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [x] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [ ] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.
author: charettes

reviews.body:
@jacobtylerwalls let me know what you think of the approach so far. The commit history obvisouly needs some rework.

We'll want to keep disallowing some characters in users provided aliases until we adjust the `quote_alias` method of each backend to transform them (e.g. handle `"` as `"""`, strip `\x00`) according to their [respective docs](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-syntax-lexical.html#SQL-SYNTAX-IDENTIFIERS)) but the current patch should at least provide a fondation to ensure we don't have ever skip calling `quote_name` on aliases.

This sparks a lot of joy. Makes total sense for a first step.

My understanding is that the rest of the plan is:
- make `quote_name_unless_alias()` a no-op for `connection.ops.quote_name()` and deprecate it
- document the need to possibly adjust casing of RawSQL expressions
- [in follow ups:] begin undoing `FORBIDDEN_ALIAS_PATTERN` by auditing the `quote_name()` methods for each backend to see what needs special handling

Do you anticipate any issues with these `.lower()` comparisons?

https://github.com/django/django/blob/2831eaed797627e6e6410b06f74dadeb63316e09/django/db/models/expressions.py#L1254-L1265
I'll look into adding the missing release notes in the next few days.
Thank you for working on this!
@charettes Thanks 👍

commits.message:
Fixed #36795 -- Enforced quoting of all database object names.

This ensures all database identifiers are quoted independently of their orign
and most importantly that user provided aliases through annotate() and alias()
which paves the way for dropping the allow list of characters such aliases can
contain.

This will require adjustments to raw SQL interfaces such as RawSQL that might
make reference to ORM managed annotations as these will now be quoted.

The `SQLCompiler.quote_name_unless_alias` method is kept for now as an alias
for the newly introduced `.quote_name` method but will be duly deprecated in
a follow up commit.

Refs #36795 -- Removed unnecessary prohibits_dollar_signs_in_column_aliases feature flag.

Now that user provided aliases are systematically quoted there is no need to
disallow the usage of the dollar sign on Postgres.

Refs #36795 -- Deprecated SQLCompiler.quote_name_unless_alias().

It has been superseded with .quote_name(), which ensures aliases are
always quoted.

title: Fixed #36847 -- Ensured auto_now_add fields are set before FileField.upload_to is called.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36847

#### Branch description
In Django 5.2 and earlier, an `auto_now_add=True` `DateTimeField` could be accessed inside a `FileField`’s `upload_to` callable, because the field was populated with the current timestamp during insertion.
This is a regression introduced in 94680437a45a71c70ca8bd2e68b72aa1e2eff337 where the insert path called `field.pre_save()` with `add=False` instead of `add=True`. 

This fix restores the correct behavior by changing the argument back to `add=True`.
(Used AI to help create the test coverage for the fix. I have verified that it's working correctly.)

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)

I pushed some edits and will merge when CI is green.

commits.message:
Fixed #36847 -- Ensured auto_now_add fields are set on pre_save().

Regression in 94680437a45a71c70ca8bd2e68b72aa1e2eff337. Refs #27222.

During INSERT operations, `field.pre_save()` is called to prepare values
for db insertion. The `add` param must be `True` for `auto_now_add`
fields to be populated. The regression commit passed `False`, causing
`auto_now_add` fields to remain `None` when used by other fields, such
as `upload_to` callables.

Thanks Ran Benita for the report.

title: Fixed #36879 -- Identified Django client in Redis client metadata.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36879

#### Branch description
Redis documentation recommends that clients identify themselves via connection metadata to help operators monitor, debug, and reason about production systems (for example using CLIENT SETINFO / CLIENT INFO).

This patch allows Django to set its own driver info for redis-py connections, which will be a different `lib-name`:

 `redis-py` -> `redis-py(django_v{django_version})`.

This is achieved by subclassing the redis-py `Connection` class to include Django's custom `lib_name` or `driver_info`.

title: Fixed #36878 -- Unified data type for *_together options in ModelState.
body:
Ever since the beginning of Django's migration framework, there's been a bit of an inconsistency on how index_together and unique_together values have been stored on the ModelState[^1].

It's only really obvious, when looking at the current code for `from_model()`[^2] and the `rename_field()` state alteration code[^3].

The problem in the autodetector's detection of the `*_together` options as raised in the ticket, reinforces the inconsistency[^4]: the old value is being normalized to a set of tuples, whereas the new value is taken as-is.

Why this hasn't been caught before, is likely to the fact, that we never really look at a `to_state` that comes from migration operations in the autodetector. Instead, in both usages in Django[^5], [^6] the `to_state` is a `ProjectState.from_apps()`. And that state is consistently using sets of tuples and not lists of lists.

[^1]: https://github.com/django/django/commit/67dcea711e92025d0e8676b869b7ef15dbc6db73#diff-5dd147e9e978e645313dd99eab3a7bab1f1cb0a53e256843adb68aeed71e61dcR85-R87
[^2]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/b1ffa9a9d78b0c2c5ad6ed5a1d84e380d5cfd010/django/db/migrations/state.py#L842
[^3]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/b1ffa9a9d78b0c2c5ad6ed5a1d84e380d5cfd010/django/db/migrations/state.py#L340-L345
[^4]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/b1ffa9a9d78b0c2c5ad6ed5a1d84e380d5cfd010/django/db/migrations/autodetector.py#L1757-L1771
[^5]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/2351c1b12cc9cf82d642f769c774bc3ea0cc4006/django/core/management/commands/makemigrations.py#L215-L219
[^6]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/2351c1b12cc9cf82d642f769c774bc3ea0cc4006/django/core/management/commands/migrate.py#L329-L332

#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36878

commits.message:
Fixed #36878 -- Unified data type for *_together options in ModelState.

Ever since the beginning of Django's migration framework, there's been a
bit of an inconsistency on how index_together and unique_together values
have been stored on the ModelState[^1].

It's only really obvious, when looking at the current code for
`from_model()`[^2] and the `rename_field()` state alteration code[^3].

The problem in the autodetector's detection of the `*_together` options
as raised in the ticket, reinforces the inconsistency[^4]: the old value
is being normalized to a set of tuples, whereas the new value is taken
as-is.

Why this hasn't been caught before, is likely to the fact, that we
never really look at a `to_state` that comes from migration operations
in the autodetector. Instead, in both usages in Django[^5], [^6] the
`to_state` is a `ProjectState.from_apps()`. And that state is
consistently using sets of tuples and not lists of lists.

[^1]: https://github.com/django/django/commit/67dcea711e92025d0e8676b869b7ef15dbc6db73#diff-5dd147e9e978e645313dd99eab3a7bab1f1cb0a53e256843adb68aeed71e61dcR85-R87
[^2]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/b1ffa9a9d78b0c2c5ad6ed5a1d84e380d5cfd010/django/db/migrations/state.py#L842
[^3]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/b1ffa9a9d78b0c2c5ad6ed5a1d84e380d5cfd010/django/db/migrations/state.py#L340-L345
[^4]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/b1ffa9a9d78b0c2c5ad6ed5a1d84e380d5cfd010/django/db/migrations/autodetector.py#L1757-L1771
[^5]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/2351c1b12cc9cf82d642f769c774bc3ea0cc4006/django/core/management/commands/makemigrations.py#L215-L219
[^6]: https://github.com/django/django/blob/2351c1b12cc9cf82d642f769c774bc3ea0cc4006/django/core/management/commands/migrate.py#L329-L332

title: Refs #36620 -- Ran coverage tests workflow on forks.
body:
#### Branch description
When 26b0e2bb92caf2d16cabe455792350f20d6f42ca added a push trigger to the windows(python) tests action, that was to limit repetitive CI minutes from running the same python test suite twice on PRs: once with coverage enabled and once without.

We can continue to limit the separate coverage _comment_ workflow to django/django, but now that the coverage _tests_ workflow is the main python test workflow, it should run on forks by default. The other tests workflow (currently running only JavaScript tests) may start running python tests again once we flesh out the matrix.

We noticed this when staging security patches in a fork.

Follow-up to 26b0e2bb92caf2d16cabe455792350f20d6f42ca.

We can continue to limit the coverage comment workflow to django/django,
but now that this workflow is the main python test workflow, it should
run on forks by default. The other tests workflow (currently running
only JavaScript tests) may start running python tests again once we flesh
out the matrix, but since it was duplicating the coverage tests configuration,

title: Fixed #36869 -- Optimized MigrationGraph._generate_plan() with set-based membership check.
body:
Optimized MigrationGraph._generate_plan() with set-based membership checking

Prior to this change, _generate_plan() used list membership testing (migration not in plan) which is O(n). With large migration graphs, this resulted in O(n²) behavior as the plan list grew during iteration.

For a large project with over 9,000 migrations and over 200 first-party apps:
- Each forwards_plan() call returns thousands of migrations
- Total iterations: 200+ apps × 4,000 migrations = 800,000+ checks
- Average plan size during checks: ~4,500 migrations
- Total comparisons: ~3.6 billion O(n) list scans

This change adds a seen set for O(1) membership checking while maintaining the ordered plan list for the return value. The set tracks which migrations have been added, eliminating the expensive list scan on each check.

Performance impact:
- Time complexity: O(nodes × migrations × plan_size) → O(nodes × migrations)
- Practical improvement: O(n²) → O(n)
- Measured speedup on large project: 95%+ reduction (20x+ faster)
- Operations reduced: billions → hundreds of thousands

The optimization maintains identical behavior:
- Plan order is preserved (insertion order maintained in list)
- No duplicates (set prevents re-insertion)
- All migration dependencies are respected (topological order from forwards_plan)

Memory overhead is minimal (~0.5MB for the seen set on large projects).

AI Tools: I have used Claude code to assist with locating the root cause of the performance issue.

#### Trac ticket number

ticket-36869

#### Branch description
Small change to reduce migration digraph construction complexity from O(n^2) to O(n).

reviews.body:
Hello! Thank you for your contribution 💪

As it's your first contribution be sure to check out the [patch review checklist](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/#patch-review-checklist).

If you're fixing a ticket [from Trac](https://code.djangoproject.com/) make sure to set the _"Has patch"_ flag and include a link to this PR in the ticket!

If you have any design or process questions then you can ask in the [Django forum](https://forum.djangoproject.com/c/internals/5).

Welcome aboard ⛵️!
Wow, nice find! Good work.

Thank you @jfysh-kraken for the analysis and PR! 🌟 

In `main`:
```
compare   100 (0.0751ms) against   200 (0.2267ms) migrations (2.0x size): 3.02x time
compare   200 (0.2267ms) against   400 (0.7694ms) migrations (2.0x size): 3.39x time
compare   400 (0.7694ms) against   800 (2.8608ms) migrations (2.0x size): 3.72x time
compare   800 (2.8608ms) against  1600 (11.1207ms) migrations (2.0x size): 3.89x time
compare  1600 (11.1207ms) against  3200 (43.3322ms) migrations (2.0x size): 3.90x time
```

With this PR:
```
compare   100 (0.0483ms) against   200 (0.0899ms) migrations (2.0x size): 1.86x time
compare   200 (0.0899ms) against   400 (0.1781ms) migrations (2.0x size): 1.98x time
compare   400 (0.1781ms) against   800 (0.3853ms) migrations (2.0x size): 2.16x time
compare   800 (0.3853ms) against  1600 (0.7352ms) migrations (2.0x size): 1.91x time
compare  1600 (0.7352ms) against  3200 (2.1157ms) migrations (2.0x size): 2.88x time
```

If I undo the `set(nodes)` change, the numbers do not vary, so I'm not sure that's worth the diff cost? Wanna verify yourself?
```
compare   100 (0.0488ms) against   200 (0.0907ms) migrations (2.0x size): 1.86x time
compare   200 (0.0907ms) against   400 (0.1748ms) migrations (2.0x size): 1.93x time
compare   400 (0.1748ms) against   800 (0.3567ms) migrations (2.0x size): 2.04x time
compare   800 (0.3567ms) against  1600 (0.7369ms) migrations (2.0x size): 2.07x time
compare  1600 (0.7369ms) against  3200 (2.0327ms) migrations (2.0x size): 2.76x time
```

But overall, wow! 8x faster to 800 migrations, 16x for 1600, and ~20x faster for 3200! :trophy:

commits.message:
Fixed #36869 -- Optimized MigrationGraph._generate_plan membership checks.

Previously, `_generate_plan()` relied on list membership checks,
resulting in quadratic behavior as the plan grew. On large migration
graphs this became a significant performance bottleneck.

This change uses `OrderedSet` for the plan, reducing the complexity to
linear while preserving insertion order and behavior.

Co-authored-by: Nick Pope <nick@nickpope.me.uk>

title: Fixed #36865 -- Removed casting from exact lookups in admin searches.
body:
Fixes https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/36865

## Description

This PR fixes a performance regression introduced in PR #17885.

The `Cast` to `CharField` for non-string field exact lookups prevents database index usage on primary key fields, generating SQL like:

```sql
WHERE ("table"."id")::varchar = '123'
```

This cannot use the primary key index and causes full table scans, resulting in timeouts on large tables (reported in [ticket #26001 comment 24](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/26001#comment:24)), and discovered in a project I work on at https://github.com/freelawproject/courtlistener/issues/6790.

## Solution

Drop the casting approach introduced in #17885, and replace it with field validation that uses `to_python` on the results of `smart_split` to ensure that we only apply field-term pairs that make sense. For example: 

 - searching a PrimaryKey field for "foo" --> Nope!
 - searching a TextField for "foo" --> Yes!
 - searching a TextField for "123" --> Close enough. Yes!

And so forth. 

This should ensure DB indexes are used and that we don't send nonsensical queries in the first place. 

## Trac tickets

Introducing issue: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/26001
Solving it: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/36865
author: mlissner

reviews.body: We'll want a dedicated ticket for that and likely a solution that isn't specific to whether a not a search field is a primary as casting to text is harmful in a lot of other cases.
Thanks @mlissner, I have initial comments 👍

Thanks for adding the test, and many thanks for being super responsive! 

One more finding for you.
Wonderful, thank you!

commits.message: Refs #36865 -- Added test for invalid value handling in admin changelist.

Fixed #36865 -- Removed casting from exact lookups in admin searches.

Instead of casting non-text fields to CharField (which prevents index
usage), skip exact lookups when the search term fails
formfield.to_python().

This preserves index usage for valid searches while gracefully handling
invalid search terms by simply not including them in the query for that
field.

For multi-term searches like 'foo 123' on search_fields=['name', 'age__exact']:
- 'foo': invalid for age, so only name lookup is used
- '123': valid for both, so both lookups are used

This entails a slight increase in permissiveness for search terms that
can be normalized by formfield.to_python().

title: Fixed #36853 -- Fixed technical 500 and 404 email CSS support.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number, or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36853

#### Branch description
The use of the :where() pseudo-class in technical_500.html caused a regression in email styling for clients with strict CSS parsers, specifically Gmail.

Gmail does not support :where(), causing it to invalidate the styling block. This resulted in exception emails rendering with a white background and broken layout instead of the expected yellow debug style.

This patch replaces the body > :where(header, main, footer) selector with standard CSS selectors supported by email clients, restoring the correct visual presentation in Gmail without affecting the browser view.

title: Refs #470 -- Fixed further field_defaults test failures due to year-end boundary conditions.
body:
Follow-up to 352d860b9107adbcde0f1fe5d0fce8e9090a51e4.

These tests involve a database default making use of `ExtractYear(Now())`, which could be sensitive to timezones around 1/1, which is what happened for the second year in a row, see [logs](https://github.com/django/django/actions/runs/20631778850/job/59250744513):

<details>
<summary>Failure</summary>

```
======================================================================
FAIL: test_bulk_create_mixed_db_defaults_function (field_defaults.tests.DefaultTests.test_bulk_create_mixed_db_defaults_function)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\hostedtoolcache\windows\Python\3.13.11\x64\Lib\unittest\case.py", line 58, in testPartExecutor
    yield
  File "C:\hostedtoolcache\windows\Python\3.13.11\x64\Lib\unittest\case.py", line 651, in run
    self._callTestMethod(testMethod)
    
  File "C:\hostedtoolcache\windows\Python\3.13.11\x64\Lib\unittest\case.py", line 606, in _callTestMethod
    if method() is not None:
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "D:\a\django\django\django\test\testcases.py", line 1588, in skip_wrapper
    return test_func(*args, **kwargs)
    ^^^^^^^
  File "D:\a\django\django\django\test\utils.py", line 458, in inner
    return func(*args, **kwargs)
    ^^^
  File "D:\a\django\django\tests\field_defaults\tests.py", line 184, in test_bulk_create_mixed_db_defaults_function
    self.assertCountEqual(years, [2000, timezone.now().year])
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "C:\hostedtoolcache\windows\Python\3.13.11\x64\Lib\unittest\case.py", line 1238, in assertCountEqual
    self.fail(msg)
    ^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "C:\hostedtoolcache\windows\Python\3.13.11\x64\Lib\unittest\case.py", line 732, in fail
    raise self.failureException(msg)
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
AssertionError: Element counts were not equal:
First has 1, Second has 0:  2025
First has 0, Second has 1:  2026

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 18919 tests in 259.254s
```
</details>

Overriding `USE_TZ=True` during a test creates drift between the SQL compiled for inserted values versus the deployed database default, as `Extract()` inquires of the current timezone:

https://github.com/django/django/blob/c68e4adea0703354508d51895b091771b1f6ac45/django/db/models/functions/datetime.py#L56-L57

To resolve this, leave `USE_TZ=False` and make UTC explicit when asserting over the result.


***
The reason only one of the two tests I touched last time in #18983 is not working is due to a behavior difference between `create()` and `bulk_create()` discussed at greater length at https://github.com/django/django/pull/18983#issuecomment-3715528301, which I will probably turn into a forum post to sound out.
author: jacobtylerwalls

reviews.body:
This looks sensible, though I stumbled with the usage of `astimezone(UTC)`. I never used this method, so I read a bit about it, but I still can't quite grasp why we would favor this instead of the more direct `datetime.now(UTC)`. Could you please share your rationale for this choice?

Thank you! 🌟

commits.message:
Refs #470 -- Fixed further field_defaults test failures due to year-end boundary conditions.

Follow-up to 352d860b9107adbcde0f1fe5d0fce8e9090a51e4.

Overriding USE_TZ=True during a test creates drift between the SQL compiled
for inserted values versus the deployed database default, as Extract() inquires
of the current timezone.

To resolve this, leave USE_TZ=False and make UTC explicit when asserting the result.

title: Made release test script use a random available port.
body:
As a releaser, if you have a utility like https://github.com/nessita/checklist-generator or anything else running by default on 8000, it's easy (happened to a couple of us) for this script to exit due to a port conflict without properly cleaning up temporary artifacts, adding a couple steps of friction to get back on the happy path (artifacts must be manually deleted).

Suggest just bumping the port to catch the obvious conflict with a Django app running on 8000.
author: jacobtylerwalls

reviews.body:
Thank you @jacobtylerwalls for this work!

I was thinking we should try random ports instead, otherwise we risk the same conflict with a hardcoded port. How about a random port above 10K?

```bash
python manage.py runserver 127.0.0.1:$(shuf -i 10000-65535 -n 1)
```
Thank you Jacob and Jake 🤩
Just perfect! 🌟

commits.message:
Made release test script use a random available port.

Co-authored-by: Jake Howard <git@theorangeone.net>

title: Fixed #35442 -- Prevented N+1 queries in RelatedManager with only().
body:
ticket-35442

#### Branch description
This PR prevents N+1 queries when iterating over a `RelatedManager` queryset that uses `.only()` to exclude the foreign key field.

Previously, `ModelIterable` would attempt to optimize the relationship population by accessing the foreign key on the child object. If that key was deferred (via .`only()`), accessing it triggered an immediate SQL query for every row.

The fix modifies `ModelIterable` to check `obj.__dict__` first. If the foreign key attributes are missing (deferred), the optimization is skipped, preserving the lazy loading behavior requested by the user.

**Tests:**
* Added `test_only_related_manager_optimization` in `defer.tests` to verify query count is 1 (previously N+1).
* Updated `defer.tests.DeferTests.test_only` to assert the correct number of deferred fields (3 instead of 2), reflecting that the foreign key is now correctly deferred.

#### Checklist
- [ ] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [ ] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period.

title: Fixed #36815 -- Optimized insertion of db_default fields in bulk_create().
body:
Avoid unnecessary prepare_value calls when inserting db_defaults.

#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number, or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36815

#### Branch description
bulk_create() was repeatedly calling prepare_value() for DatabaseDefault values on every inserted object, even though those defaults are later discarded or handled once by the backend.
This caused calling unnecessary prepare_value() and significant overhead for large bulk inserts.
The fix skips per-object preparation for DatabaseDefault, preserving behavior while avoiding redundant work.

title: Fixed #36829 -- Revert unintended ClearableFileInput.use_fieldset change
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-36829
Ref 4187da2

#### Branch description

Revert undocumented flip of `ClearableFileInput.use_fieldset` in Django 6.0.

#### Checklist
- [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period.
- [x] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system.
- [x] I have added or updated relevant tests.
- [x] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable.
- [x] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: codingjoe

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR reverts an unintended change in Django 6.0 where the `use_fieldset` attribute of `ClearableFileInput` was incorrectly changed from `False` to `True`. The fix restores the original behavior while ensuring the admin widget maintains its expected behavior.

**Key changes:**
- Reverted `ClearableFileInput.use_fieldset` from `True` back to `False`
- Added explicit `use_fieldset = True` to `AdminFileWidget` to preserve admin-specific rendering
- Updated test expectations to reflect the corrected label-based rendering instead of fieldset-based rendering

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| `django/forms/widgets.py` | Reverts `ClearableFileInput.use_fieldset` from `True` to `False`, restoring the original behavior |
| `django/contrib/admin/widgets.py` | Explicitly sets `use_fieldset = True` on `AdminFileWidget` to maintain admin-specific fieldset rendering |
| `tests/forms_tests/widget_tests/test_clearablefileinput.py` | Updates test expectations from fieldset/legend elements to label elements, and verifies `use_fieldset` is `False` |
| `docs/releases/6.0.1.txt` | Documents the regression fix in the 6.0.1 release notes |
Thanks for the report and fix!

commits.message:
Fixed #36829 -- Reverted value of ClearableFileInput.use_fieldset to True.

There was unresolved discussion regarding whether to set
ClearableFileInput.use_fieldset to True or False when use_fieldset was
introduced in Django 4.1, since the clear checkbox appears only
sometimes. Although using <fieldset> is likely desirable, since the
primary motivation in #35892 was just to improve markup in the admin,
and a deprecation path was not provided for general form usage, future
work is deferred to #36828.

Regression in 4187da258fe212d494cb578a0bc2b52c4979ab95.

Thanks Tim Graham, Antoliny, and David Smith for triage.

title: Fixed #36600 -- Clarified dispatch_uid usage for signal receivers.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
ticket-36600

#### Branch description
Clarifies the usage of `dispatch_uid` for signal receivers in Django. The current documentation may mislead users to think that `dispatch_uid` is always required. This update explains the actual scenarios where `dispatch_uid` is necessary, highlights Django's internal deduplication logic, and provides clearer examples to guide developers.

#### Checklist
- [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period.

reviews.body:
Hello! Thank you for your contribution 💪

If you have any design or process questions then you can ask in the [Django forum](https://forum.djangoproject.com/c/internals/5).

Welcome aboard ⛵️!
Thanks for the updates, just a couple suggested tweaks.

Thanks 👍 

I tweaked the example to hopefully add a little realism around how you would end up with duplicates, e.g. calling a check() method that instantiates a new backend object, following an example in Django itself. I also softened the wording to not advise to always do this for _every_ bound method, as sometimes the additional registrations are wanted, if the objects are different in a substantial way (different backends).

body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-36827

#### Checklist

author: hakib

reviews.body:
Looking at c2f6c05c4cc73e831b7e852eb58bd6d7a83fa46c which added SP-GiST support the only part that might have been missing here was to confirm that `main` which is Postgres 15+ supports it with all options.

Vanilla support [is oviously the case](https://dbfiddle.uk/Rp5Vk5XG) as `HashIndex` has been around forever (Django 2.2) so this was most likely an omission when we added `ExclusionConstraint` but [it's not the case](https://dbfiddle.uk/OunMZxrA) for covering indices (see `include` option) which we'll need to add a check for [like we use to do](https://github.com/django/django/commit/b049bec7cfe9b5854584d240addb44fa1e9375a5#diff-a4e33792faf48c1d998ea65d49b65a6ab695efad8c2b74891180e055342e92eeL117-L141) and [tested](https://github.com/django/django/commit/b049bec7cfe9b5854584d240addb44fa1e9375a5#diff-9bcfdc1360b06330a51e54b7565748f9ef23685b7ac2983d7b390bd93e7f71e1L1087-L1107) before we dropped support for Postgres 14.
@hakib Thanks :+1: I pushed tiny final edits.

title: Fixed #36644 -- Enabled empty order_by() to avoid pk ordering by first()/last().
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number, or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-36644

#### Branch description
Enabled calling `order_by()` without arguments to disable implicit primary key ordering in `first()`/`last()`. When a queryset is explicitly marked as unordered via `order_by()`, `first()`/`last()` now respect this and avoid adding PK ordering. Also reset default_ordering in `_combinator_query()` so that `union().first()` can still add pk ordering.

Discussion: https://github.com/django/new-features/issues/71, 


#### Checklist

title: Fixed #36818 -- Ensured SQLite connection before accessing max_query_params.
body:
Ensure that the SQLite database connection is established before accessing
DatabaseFeatures.max_query_params.

This fixes a regression where calling QuerySet.bulk_create() as the first
database operation after django.setup() could raise an AttributeError on
SQLite due to an uninitialized connection.

#### Trac ticket number
ticket-36818

#### Branch description
SQLite's DatabaseFeatures.max_query_params accessed the underlying DB-API
connection without ensuring it was initialized. This change calls
ensure_connection() before querying the SQLite variable limit, preventing
AttributeError when backend features are accessed prior to any SQL execution.

#### Checklist

title: Refs #28455 -- Implemented private API methods for preventing QuerySet cloning.
body:
Multiple calls are idempotent assuming they're balanced. Also, multiple calls to disable cloning followed by a single call to re-enable cloning will subsequently cause clones to occur - it is not a stack, just a toggle.

`@contextlib.contextmanager` is intentionally not used for performance reasons:
- decorator takes 1.1µs to execute, or 2µs if used correctly in a `with ...:` statement
- custom class takes 300ns to execute, or 900ns if used correctly in a `with ...:` statement

Based on work originally done by Anssi Kääriäinen and Tim Graham.

ticket-28455

I've rebased #14675 with a tiny edits.
author: felixxm

reviews.body:
This is looking promising @felixxm thank you for pushing this forward and sorry for being so slow to review.

I'm not intimately familiary with why we chose `_chain` as the injection point over `_clone` but assuming there is one good reason for it might be better to name the API `_inplace` instead of `_avoid_cloning` as `_clone` very much still work?

While I'm not entirely sold on the naming here I think it's a good enough solution to allow us to experiment internally with avoiding to perform unnecessary queryset cloning.

I'd hope we could entirely disable cloning instead (making `clone` a noop) but the weird interactions with cloning and chaining (with the even weirder sticky filters heritage) makes this quite tricky.

Thanks for pushing this through @felixxm!

commits.message:
Refs #28455 -- Implemented private API methods for preventing QuerySet cloning.

Multiple calls are idempotent assuming they're balanced. Also, multiple
calls to disable cloning followed by a single call to re-enable cloning
will subsequently cause clones to occur - it is not a stack, just a
toggle.

@contextlib.contextmanager is intentionally not used for performance
reasons:
- decorator takes 1.1µs to execute, or 2µs if used correctly in a
  `with ...:` statement
- custom class takes 300ns to execute, or 900ns if used correctly in a
  `with ...:` statement

Based on work originally done by Anssi Kääriäinen and Tim Graham.

Refs #28455 -- Avoided QuerySet cloning in simple prefetch_related() usages.

manager.get_queryset() always returns freshly instantiated per-instance
QuerySet which doesn't need subsequent cloning.

Based on work originally done by Anssi Kääriäinen and Tim Graham.

Refs #28455 -- Avoided QuerySet cloning for Prefetch() when queryset is not provided.

Co-authored-by: Mariusz Felisiak <felisiak.mariusz@gmail.com>

Hello! Thank you for your contribution 💪

As it's your first contribution be sure to check out the [patch review checklist](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/#patch-review-checklist).

If you have any design or process questions then you can ask in the [Django forum](https://forum.djangoproject.com/c/internals/5).

As mentioned in the ticket feedback, I think we can leave the release blocker status set ion the ticket and ensure that this PR has two commits:

* one with the fix for the underlying cause in `SimpleLazyObject` and its test (no backport)
* one with a workaround for `LazyNone` with a release note so we backport to 6.0

Thank you @sean-reed! I pushed some cleanups, in particular it occurred to me that the fix for `LazyNonce` could be done by just moving the bound method to be a function, since the nonce generation does not depend of the instance itself.

Moved nonce generation in ``django.utils.csp.LazyNonce`` to a function
to avoid infinite recursion in ``SimpleLazyObject.__repr__`` for
unevaluated instances.

Detect when `SimpleLazyObject._setupfunc` is a bound method of the same
instance to use a safe representation and avoid infinite recursion.
