reviews.body:
Hello! Thank you for your contribution 💪

As it's your first contribution be sure to check out the [patch review checklist](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/#patch-review-checklist).

If you're fixing a ticket [from Trac](https://code.djangoproject.com/) make sure to set the _"Has patch"_ flag and include a link to this PR in the ticket!

If you have any design or process questions then you can ask in the [Django forum](https://forum.djangoproject.com/c/internals/5).

Welcome aboard ⛵️!

Thank you @xuhdev for this contribution. In general, we avoid minor wording changes in the Django documentation since they can have a significant impact on translations and the git history. However, in this case, I agree that using _email address_ is more accurate and aligns with the wording used in the `models.EmailField` and `forms.EmailField` docs.

I'll backport this to `stable/6.0.x`, as it is still in beta, but I won't backport to 5.2 to avoid unnecessary translation invalidation.

Updated the EmailValidator docs in docs/ref/validators.txt to explicitly
state that it validates an email address, to avoid confusion with
validating email message content.

title: Extended the GitHub pull request template to require AI assistance disclosure.
body:
_[Updated template to show example below]_

#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-XXXXX

#### Branch description
Provide a concise overview of the issue or rationale behind the proposed changes.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [ ] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

#### Checklist
- [ ] This PR follows the [contribution guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/).
- [ ] This PR **does not** disclose a security vulnerability (see [vulnerability reporting](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/security/)).
- [ ] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [ ] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period.
- [ ] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system.
- [ ] I have added or updated relevant tests.
- [ ] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable.
- [ ] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: nessita

reviews.body:
I like the paragraph "Unverified AI-generated contributions create unnecessary maintenance burden..." as we can link to this on PRs (even tickets) on closure

> I am not confident that more steps will stop slop contributions being made, given how often folks delete or ignore the template.

I think I agree with this.
One of the biggest tell for me that something is AI-generated is that the PR description has been completely replaced with a long thing with headers
I have added some suggestions to the template if we want to mention AI content

Seems like a step in the right direction! Tested with the "init" commands of Claude Code, OpenAI Codex (2 times), Warp, and a custom prompt with Mistral Vibe.

- None would read `docs/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches.txt`. I’m not sure if this is because they got enough context without going so deep in the folder structure, or if "submitting-patches" isn’t clear enough of a file name for them.
- OpenAI Codex and Claude Code definitely read the PR template. Mistral Vibe I’m not sure. Warp didn’t.
- All / almost all make it possible to check which files are read if we wanted to know for sure.
- All except Warp have relevant guidelines in their output which indicate they understand the request.

Here’s the relevant excerpt of their respective generated agent guidelines for ref, [full generated guidelines available in a gist](https://gist.github.com/thibaudcolas/6073ab19995fa71f67e60f7f88307cfe):

<details>

<summary>OpenAI Codex output</summary>

> - PRs target `main` and fill `.github/pull_request_template.md`: include ticket number (or N/A), branch description, AI assistance disclosure, and checklist items.

</details>

<details>

<summary>Warp output</summary>

The `.github/pull_request_template.md` file wasn’t detected.

</details>

<details>

<summary>Claude Code output</summary>

> ### AI-Assisted Contributions
> 
> When using AI tools (including Claude Code):
> - You must disclose AI tool usage in the PR template
> - You must fully review and verify all AI-generated output
> - Ensure code aligns with Django's architecture and passes the full test suite
> - Unverified AI contributions may be closed without review

</details>

<details>

<summary>Mistral Vibe output</summary>

> ## Agent Contributions
> 
> When submitting code that was generated or significantly modified by an agent:
> 
> 1. Clearly indicate which parts were agent-assisted
> 2. Provide information about the agent used
> 3. Ensu

Thanks @nessita!

(edited for clarity):
The added burden for each kind of contributor amounts to either checking a box by hand or [configuring their LLM](https://xeiaso.net/notes/2025/assisted-by-footer/) to comply with the policy. I think we can live with that.

We can tweak this as we go. I'll need to start preparing my own canned responses to point to parts of this and hotkey them 👍 

:shipit: !

commits.message: Extended GitHub pull request template to require AI assistance disclosure.
Added guidance on AI-assisted code submission to contributing docs.

reviews.body: This change looks good to me. I agree with @nessita that it'd be nice to reuse `log_response`, but it's so coupled to Django's `HttpResponse` that that's probably not possible. We could separate them out and have some common base which handles both Django's `HttpResponse` and this other format, but that's probably more work than it's worth - especially as the sanitizing is a single (ish) line.
I had a feeling this is what you had in mind. Seeing the diff, I agree it's worth extracting `log_message` into a util - I'm sure we'll find other places in future it could be useful.

Migrated `WSGIRequestHandler.log_message()` to use a more robust
`log_message()` helper, which was based of `log_response()` via factoring out
the common bits.

Refs CVE-2025-48432.

title: Fixed #36420 -- Used actual SQLite limits in last_executed_query() quoting.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
ticket-36420

#### Branch description
Replaced the hardcoded batch size in `last_executed_query()` for SQLite with a dynamic limit based on `sqlite3.Connection.getlimit()` (Python 3.11+). This ensures that parameter quoting does not exceed SQLite’s runtime constraints, avoiding `OperationalError` on systems with custom-compiled SQLite (e.g., with increased `SQLITE_MAX_VARIABLE_NUMBER`).

This change is safe because Django 6.0 and later versions have dropped support for Python 3.10 and 3.11 (#36005), and thus `getlimit()` (introduced in Python 3.11) is always available.

#### Checklist

title: Fixed #34041 -- Improve accessibility of admin page breadcrumbs area.
body:
#### Trac ticket number ticket-34041 #### Branch description [Continue farita1699's PR](https://github.com/django/django/pull/16414) I have improved the accessibility of the breadcrumb area in the admin page as follows. 1. I updated the breadcrumb links to be structured as `
  • ` items within an `
      ` list. This change allows screen reader users to know how many items are present in the breadcrumbs area. 2. I added aria-current="page" to the element representing the current page in the breadcrumb area. This change allows screen reader users to identify the current page by announcing it as the "current page" when navigating to the corresponding breadcrumb item. 3. I added an `` tag to the last item in the breadcrumbs. This change makes the last breadcrumb item accessible via the "Tab key" when using a screen reader. #### Checklist - [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. - [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period. - [x] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system. - [x] I have added or updated relevant tests. - [ ] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable. - [ ] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.

title: Fixed #13883 -- Rendered named choice groups with <optgroup> in FilteredSelectMultiple.
body: #### Trac ticket number ticket-13883 #### Branch description The following had already been partially implemented with https://github.com/django/django/pull/15568: - Modified SelectBox.js so that optgroups are honored. - When an item is moved from unselected to selected box, order is maintained by group and then item name. - Also added a test. I forked this [example project](https://github.com/reergymerej/ticket_13883) and made the following updates in [a branch](https://github.com/seanhelvey/ticket_13883), because a few small things were causing errors and confusion in the track discussion: - Updated ProfileForm with model and fields. - Used tuple (id, name) instead of (name, name) for choices. - Changed unicode method to str in Sport model. Now that the Sport model uses the str method instead of unicode, the following change in comparison was needed: From `if choice_obj == obj:` to `if choice_obj == str(obj):` (in response_add below). Otherwise most of the changes in the original PR and example project had been completed already. #### Checklist - [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. - [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period. - [x] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system. - [x] I have added or updated relevant tests. - [x] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable. - [x] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: seanhelvey

reviews.body:
Hello! Thank you for your contribution 💪

As it's your first contribution be sure to check out the [patch review checklist](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/#patch-review-checklist).

If you're fixing a ticket [from Trac](https://code.djangoproject.com/) make sure to set the _"Has patch"_ flag and include a link to this PR in the ticket!

If you have any design or process questions then you can ask in the [Django forum](https://forum.djangoproject.com/c/internals/5).

Welcome aboard ⛵️!

Thank you for the PR!

I was testing this by changing the User permissions to have option groups:
```diff
--- a/django/contrib/auth/forms.py
+++ b/django/contrib/auth/forms.py
@@ -1,14 +1,19 @@
 import logging
 import unicodedata
+from functools import partial
+from itertools import groupby
+from operator import attrgetter
 
 from django import forms
+from django.contrib.admin.widgets import FilteredSelectMultiple
 from django.contrib.auth import authenticate, get_user_model, password_validation
 from django.contrib.auth.hashers import UNUSABLE_PASSWORD_PREFIX, identify_hasher
-from django.contrib.auth.models import User
+from django.contrib.auth.models import User, Permission
 from django.contrib.auth.tokens import default_token_generator
 from django.contrib.sites.shortcuts import get_current_site
 from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError
 from django.core.mail import EmailMultiAlternatives
+from django.forms.models import ModelChoiceIterator
 from django.template import loader
 from django.utils.encoding import force_bytes
 from django.utils.http import urlsafe_base64_encode
@@ -289,6 +294,30 @@ class UserCreationForm(BaseUserCreationForm):
             return username
 
 
+class GroupedModelChoiceIterator(ModelChoiceIterator):
+    def __init__(self, field, groupby):
+        self.groupby = groupby
+        super().__init__(field)
+
+    def __iter__(self):
+        queryset = self.queryset
+        # Can't use iterator() when queryset uses prefetch_related()
+        if not queryset._prefetch_related_lookups:
+            queryset = queryset.iterator()
+        for group, objs in groupby(queryset, self.groupby):
+            yield (group, [self.choice(obj) for obj in objs])
+
+
+class GroupedModelChoiceField(forms.ModelMultipleChoiceField):
+    def __init__(self, *args, choices_groupby, **kwargs):
+        if isinstance(choices_groupby, str):
+            choices_groupby = attrgetter(choices_groupby)
+

Hey @seanhelvey, thanks for the updates.

I just got around to checking the test coverage. The most complex part of the patch doesn't have a test.

Once that's in, could you also please add the prior author in the commit message with "Co-authored-by: "?

Adding ".patch" to the commit URL will show the exact signature to use:

https://github.com/django/django/commit/18f0c818abaf4daca42d734d9b28397045437677.patch

Thanks!
Thanks @seanhelvey 👍 . Starting to wrap my head around how to test this. One finding for you.

Thanks @seanhelvey for your patience  🎉 

These are my last comments, if I've totally exhausted you let me know and I should be able to push this over the line for you.

The last real substantive question is about whether we should be adjusting the sort behavior in this PR or not when optgroups are not in play.

Amazing. The last pushes adjusting sort behavior seem to introduce a new change. When use the + popup to create a new item belonging to group already in the "to" box, it doesn't get consolidated, appearing instead at the bottom: I would have expected my "ddd" permission to appear in the second group.
Wonderful, thanks @seanhelvey! Look at that 1xxxx ticket being closed 🎉

commits.message:
Fixed #13883 -- Rendered named choice groups with <optgroup> in FilteredSelectMultiple.

This patch adds support for <optgroup>s in FilteredSelectMultiple widgets.
When a popup returns a new object, if the source field contains optgroup
choices, the optgroup is now also included in the response data.

Additionally, this adds error handling for invalid source_model parameters
to prevent crashes and display user-friendly error messages instead.

Co-authored-by: Michael McLarnon <mmclar@gmail.com>

title: Fixed selenium tests in Firefox.
body:
Unlike Chromium-based browsers, Firefox does not automatically scroll elements into view when using the Actions API with `move_to_element`. This calls the `scrollIntoView` function explicitly, which fixes some selenium tests when not running in headless mode.

Namely, the `admin_views.tests.SeleniumTests.test_prepopulated_fields` test has a page larger than the viewport, and some elements are hidden, leading the test failing with an Exception like:
```
selenium.common.exceptions.MoveTargetOutOfBoundsException: Message: Move target (570, 968) is out of bounds of viewport dimensions (1280, 955)
```
author: pbratkowski

Fixed selenium tests in Firefox.

Unlike Chromium-based browsers, Firefox does not automatically scroll
elements into view when using the Actions API with move_to_element.
This calls the scrollIntoView function explicitly, which fixes
some selenium tests when not running in headless mode.

title: Fixed #28586 -- Added model field fetching modes.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-28586

#### Branch description

Add `QuerySet.fetch_mode()` to set the fetch mode, controlling how on-demand field loading occurs. Include three fetch modes, `FETCH_ONE` (default, existing behaviour), `FETCH_PEERS`, and `RAISE`.

#### Checklist

reviews.body:
👋 @adamchainz I just want to start by saying thank you for the continued work here, this is really looking promising 🙇 

I discussed an idea on how defining a form of fetching _protocol_ (like how _resolvable_ w/ `resolve_expression` and _compilable_ w/ `as_sql` work) to avoid unnecessary closures and keep fetcher modes to the strict minimum which is to receive an instance, a fetcher, and route the call to the fetcher.

There also seems to be a missing usage of fetch mode in `ReverseOneToOneDescriptor.__get__` but maybe this is expected for now as this is still a WIP.

Lastly I left some comments with regards to naming. I'm sure others will have opinions as well but it felt like `django.db.models.fetching` and `fetching_mode` vs `lazy` might be a better .

Thank you Simon, thoughtful as always. (P.S. just finished your Django Chat episode yesterday, nice one!). I’ve replied inline to your comments and will work on an update.

> There also seems to be a missing usage of fetch mode in `ReverseOneToOneDescriptor.__get__` but maybe this is expected for now as this is still a WIP.

Yes, this needs doing, as does `GenericForeignKey`.
Amazing work! This looks great! Me, @Majsvaffla, @nip3o and @saeldion have made this review together. We are very much looking forward having fetch modes in Django.
I left a few comments about  the global state fetcher approach, `__set_name__` manual calls, systematic creation of `peers: list[weakref]`.
Thanks @adamchainz this looks great! I left a small thought

Thanks for pushing this forward and sorry for the delay in review @adamchainz.

I left a few comments mainly requesting clarifications and more verbose commit message but in essence the state of this PR is looking great to me 🥇 🙇
I'm looking forward to this one @adamchainz. Couple of usage / roadmap questions for you.

title: Fixed #36973 -- Made fields.E348 detect accessor and manager name clashes for relationships across different models.
body:
ticket-36973 ticket-22977

Previously the check only identified the clash if it happened in a self-referential relationship. Trying to make it work across different models made it easier to identify the issue reported in ticket-36973.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [ ] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [x] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

Used Claude for initial review.

#### Checklist
- [x] This PR follows the [contribution guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/).
- [x] This PR **does not** disclose a security vulnerability (see [vulnerability reporting](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/security/)).
- [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period (see [guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/committing-code/#committing-guidelines)).
- [x] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system.
- [x] I have added or updated relevant tests.
- [ ] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable.
- [ ] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: cliffordgama

reviews.body:
@cliffordgama Thanks for working on this. 👍  Changing the check to compare against managers on the target model seems like a good direction.

That said, the current implementation looks like it can introduce false positives.

The case I tested locally is where two different models (`ModelA` and `ModelB`) both have ForeignKeys to the same `Target` model. `ModelA` uses `related_name='foo'`, which clashes with the `Target.foo manager`, while `ModelB` uses `related_name='bar'`, which should not clash.

However, with the current patch, running `ModelB.check()` also incorrectly raises `E348` for `ModelB.other`. Since it iterates over `related_opts.related_objects`, it ends up looking at all reverse relations on the target side, so it picks up `ModelA`'s accessor as well and treats it as if it were a problem with `ModelB.other`.

So here, rather than iterating over all related objects, it seems more appropriate to check only the accessor for the current field directly. In other words, comparing `self.remote_field.accessor_name` against the target model's managers should be enough. `_check_clashes()` handles this in a similar way as well ([around line 266](https://github.com/cliffordgama/django/blob/e671beb8da297586221ad1b37afa8109345c3ba0/django/db/models/fields/related.py#L266)).

```diff
def _check_conflict_with_managers(self):
    errors = []
-    manager_names = {manager.name for manager in self.opts.managers}
-    for rel_objs in self.model._meta.related_objects:
-        related_object_name = rel_objs.name
-        if related_object_name in manager_names:
+    from django.db.models.base import ModelBase
+
+    if not isinstance(self.remote_field.model, ModelBase):
+        return errors
+    if self.remote_field.hidden:
+        return errors
+
+    accessor = self.remote_field.accessor_name
+    manager_names = {m.name for m in self.related_model._meta.managers}
+    if accessor in manager_names:
        field_name = f"{self.model._meta.object_name}.{self.name}"

commits.message:
Fixed #36973 -- Made fields.E348 check detect further clashes between managers and related_names.

Clashes were only detected for self-referential relationships, i.e. ForeignKey("self").

Refs #22977. Bug in 6888375c53476011754f778deabc6cdbfa327011.

Thanks JaeHyuckSa for the thorough review!

title: Fixed #20024 -- Fixed handling of __in lookups with None in exclude().
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number, or delete the line and write "N/A" if this is a trivial PR. -->

ticket-20024

#### Branch description
**Problem**
The SQL generated by an exclude queryset is not semantically aligned with the queryset when using `__in` lookups containing `None`.
```python
>>> str(Entry.objects.exclude(foo__in=[None, 1]).query)
'SELECT "core_entry"."id", "core_entry"."foo" FROM "core_entry" WHERE NOT ("core_entry"."foo" IN (1) AND "core_entry"."foo" IS NOT NULL)'
```
**Solution**
The PR updates `WhereNode` to use `OR` and generate `IS NULL` for correct SQL semantics in this case.
```python
>>> str(Entry.objects.exclude(foo__in=[None, 1]).query)
'SELECT "core_entry"."id", "core_entry"."foo" FROM "core_entry" WHERE NOT (("core_entry"."foo" IN (1) OR "core_entry"."foo" IS NULL))'
```

#### Checklist
- [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period.

title: Fixed #37004 -- Used QuerySet.totally_ordered in BaseModelFormSet.get_queryset() for stable ordering.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
<!-- Replace XXXXX with the corresponding Trac ticket number. -->
<!-- Or delete the line and write "N/A - typo" for typo fixes. -->

ticket-37004

#### Branch description
Uses `QuerySet.totally_ordered` on `BaseModelFormSet` to ensure stable ordering.

Solution implemented is the same used in [Serializers](https://github.com/django/django/blob/edd899786851f6285abbc3c272f4f5ec0e48a74c/django/core/serializers/python.py#L82-L87): detect when the ordering is not stable/deterministic (totally_ordered is false), and if so append `"pk"` to the current ordering to have a stable tie-breaker.

Thanks @charettes for pointing out the best approach to implement this, and to @jacobtylerwalls for the review.

title: Refs #36770 -- Guarded against an endless wait in LiveServerThread.terminate().
body:
#### Trac ticket number
ticket-36770

#### Branch description
On Python 3.14, the default multiprocessing mode on Linux is now "forkserver". When running tests in parallel, this has put stress on the parts of Django's test suite involving threads and in-memory SQLite databases, as described in the ticket.

This PR proposes:
- adding safety to `LiveServerTestCase` to prevent endless waits
- ~adding safety to `test_in_memory_database_lock()` to reduce the likelihood of deadlocks from "accidental" concurrency with other tests. It's possible this is treating the symptom and not the cause, but the issue has been causing pain for almost an entire feature release, so I'd like to at least land some mitigation.~ EDIT: after several pushes, we still encountered intermittent failures, so I removed this from the PR.

I anticipate leaving the ticket open until:
- we address the other flaking test, `test_database_sharing_in_threads()`, unless it turns out that this was the very test that was interfering with `test_in_memory_database_lock()`, and it stops failing, too. Knowing that will help us advance the investigation.
- we decide whether to run the frozen Django/"byte-compiled" job in a multiprocessing mode other than `forkserver` (or without parallelism at all), as [warned](https://docs.python.org/3/library/multiprocessing.html#contexts-and-start-methods) in the Python docs. (I don't want to rush that change in just yet, because that's the CI job that's been failing/hanging most often, so I'd like to leave it on after landing this first burst of fixes to observe the effects.)

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
<!-- Please select exactly ONE of the following: -->
- [x] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [ ] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

title: [5.2.x] Bumped black to 26.3.1.
body:
- black fixed a security issue in their Github Action in 26.3.0, affecting a feature we do not use (see: https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-v53h-f6m7-xcgm)
- This was causing zizmor failures (known vulnerable action) on 5.2 and 4.2
- Django 4.2 is only in extended support for another week
- Django 5.2 has two years to run
- Proposal: bump black to a patched version on Django 5.2
  - This required backporting the changes to adopt 2026's stable style, which I did in a separate commit, and could be git-ignored after the rebase & merge.
- Proposal: ignore 4.2
- Proposal: ignore 6.0 & main, continue to leave them unpinned, revisit when adopting 2027 stable style
author: jacobtylerwalls

reviews.body: LGTM! Thank you 🌟

title: Fixed #29762 -- Document how database routers are used for related-object access.
body:
#### Trac ticket number

ticket-29762

#### Branch description
This PR documents how Django selects the database for related-object access in multi-database configurations.
Django records the originating database on ``instance._state.db`` and uses it for related lookups.
When a database router overrides ``db_for_read()`` without considering the ``instance`` hint, this default behavior is lost, which can cause errors in multi-schema setups.
The patch adds a section explaining the default behavior, the use of ``hints``, and how routers should preserve correct routing.

#### Checklist
- [x] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [x] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period.
- [x] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system.
- [ ] I have added or updated relevant tests.
- [x] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable.
- [ ] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: VIZZARD-X

reviews.body: Yes I agree that reads well now.

Thanks @VIZZARD-X  for the PR and thanks @blighj for the review.

I appreciate @blighj's remarks about this being niche, so I tried to minimize the footprint of the changes even more. IMO it's a little early in this doc for a code example. (I think it steals thunder from the example below, which is introduced carefully with elaborate disclaimers.)

Please note that "proper" is a boilerplate word to avoid. We also don't know if it's _proper_ to ensure related objects are fetched from the same db; the example below uses replicas and doesn't follow this advice. We can state it more neutrally by just mentioning Django's default behavior.

Yeah I think that should steer someone in the right direction and if they did miss it and encounter the error on the ticket, it should give them or someone helping them on the forum etc enough to go on to solve their issue.

Anyone messing with multiple databases must have a fair idea of what the are doing already.

commits.message:
Fixed #29762 -- Doc'd how database routers fetch related objects.

Thanks James Bligh for the review.

Co-authored-by: Jacob Walls <jacobtylerwalls@gmail.com>

title: Refs #36526 -- Fixed bulk_update() batching example in docs.
body:
#### Trac ticket number
N/A

#### Branch description
The docs example for efficient usage of `bulk_update` has a bug creating an infinite loop where `ids` always gets the first 100 items.

#### AI Assistance Disclosure (REQUIRED)
- [X] **No AI tools were used** in preparing this PR.
- [ ] **If AI tools were used**, I have disclosed which ones, and fully reviewed and verified their output.

#### Checklist
- [X] This PR follows the [contribution guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/).
- [X] This PR **does not** disclose a security vulnerability (see [vulnerability reporting](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/internals/security/)).
- [X] This PR targets the `main` branch. <!-- Backports will be evaluated and done by mergers, when necessary. -->
- [X] The commit message is written in past tense, mentions the ticket number, and ends with a period (see [guidelines](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/committing-code/#committing-guidelines)).
- [X] I have not requested, and will not request, an automated AI review for this PR. <!-- You are welcome to do so in your own fork. -->
- [ ] I have checked the "Has patch" ticket flag in the Trac system.
- [ ] I have added or updated relevant tests.
- [X] I have added or updated relevant docs, including release notes if applicable.
- [ ] I have attached screenshots in both light and dark modes for any UI changes.
author: verigak

reviews.body:
Hello! Thank you for your contribution 💪

As it's your first contribution be sure to check out the [patch review checklist](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/writing-code/submitting-patches/#patch-review-checklist).

If you're fixing a ticket [from Trac](https://code.djangoproject.com/) make sure to set the _"Has patch"_ flag and include a link to this PR in the ticket!

If you have any design or process questions then you can ask in the [Django forum](https://forum.djangoproject.com/c/internals/5).

Welcome aboard ⛵️!

Nice catch! And thanks for making the fix. This change looks good to me.

A small part of me wonders if the fact this error slipped through in the first place indicates the example isn’t as simple as it could be. Maybe something like this would be better:
```
for i in range(0, 1000, batch_size): 
     ids = list(range(i, min(i + batch_size, 1000))
```
The example is fine either way though, and thinking out loud - I'm not actually sure my proposed solution is any better.  

Also - you can add your name to the contributors list as part of this PR too.
Thanks!

commits.message: Fixed bulk_update example in docs.
